hypothesis: Opening paragraphs that lead with a locally-specific seasonal moment or Northern Ontario reader identity statement — before naming the product — will score higher than product-first openings. Evidence: baseline drafts were technically correct but hooks were generic. Hypothesis: 'First boat of the season on St. Marys River.' or 'Sault Ste. Marie gets 290 cm of snow a year.' pulls the reader into their own world first, then delivers the product as the solution. Target: 8+/10.
learning: All 7 Reyco blog drafts produced under this surface applied the identity/seasonal-first hook rule. Docks/Lifts: 'No two Northern Ontario waterfronts are the same.' — strongest hook in the set, immediate reader recognition. Princecraft: anglers-talking-to-anglers framing before product name. Mercury: decision-stakes context before any product mention. All openings introduce the reader's situation or identity before the product. Compared to baseline (product-description openers, score 7): hooks are noticeably stronger and more engaging. No banned phrases, Canadian English, local geography (St. Marys River, Batchawana Bay, Algoma district, Lake Superior) consistent throughout. CTAs verified. Named expert (Casey Davieaux) in all bylines. Remaining gap to 9: opening lines are identity-first but could be even more specific — a single verifiable local fact (snowfall figure, ice-out date, water depth) as the lede would sharpen further. Score 8/10.
changes_description: Updated surface to require identity/seasonal-first opening hooks before product introduction. All other GLV brand voice rules unchanged. Next drafts will open with a locally-specific Northern Ontario moment before naming the product.
